schools.summary: Keil dates the episode to mid-reign and reads it as David's act of covenant gratitude at the very height of his power.
Henry: David had too long forgotten Jonathan, yet good men seek occasions to do good; 'the kindness of God' is covenant-kindness modelled on God's mercy.
Clarke blames reasons of state for the delay; Wesley says David simply did not know a son of Jonathan survived.
Gill: David speaks to his courtiers, seeking any of Saul's house yet living.

debates.question: Why did David wait so long to seek out Saul's house?

sides.position: He had forgotten the obligation, and only now remembers it
Reasons of state and political caution held him back
He did not know that any son of Jonathan was still living

themes: gratitude at the height of power

schools.summary: Keil: at Ziba's word David sends for Mephibosheth to restore his lands and seat him at the royal table, meeting him with 'Fear not.'
Gill: Ziba a retainer of Saul's house; Jewish tradition makes him a Canaanite bondman, though Josephus says Saul had freed him.
Clarke: 'the kindness of God' is the utmost degree of kindness (as 'hail of God,' 'mountains of God') and kindness in keeping with the covenant.
Gill: David asks whether any of Saul's house is yet remaining or living.
Clarke: Lodebar lay beyond Jordan, its site now uncertain — a fit hiding-place for the forgotten heir.
Gill: David asks in what part of the land Mephibosheth dwells.
Wesley: Machir sheltered Mephibosheth from compassion, not disloyalty, and later aided David against Absalom — so be forward to give, not knowing our own future need.
Gill: David's messengers, perhaps Ziba himself, are sent to fetch the heir.

themes: giving in view of future need
the king sends for the heir

schools.summary: Keil: Mephibosheth (Meribbaal) falls on his face in deepest obeisance, confessing unworthiness, and David reassures him with 'Fear not.'
Henry: as David once bowed to Jonathan next the throne, so Jonathan's son now does homage — respect shown in low estate is repaid when one is raised.
Gill: notes the double lineage, son of Jonathan and grandson of Saul, elsewhere called Meribbaal.
Clarke: restoring the land was justice (his by Israelite law), but the perpetual seat at the table was the kindness — the highest honour, echoed in Luke 22:30.
Gill: David's 'Fear not' meets the dejection and trembling of one of the seed royal who feared to be cut off.
Henry: though a prince's son, he calls himself a dead dog, magnifying grace rather than claiming it as a debt (cf. David's 'who am I?').
Wesley: it is good to have the heart humbled under humbling providences; when grace brings the spirit down with the condition, we are at ease.
Gill: the bow is at once gratitude, humility, and a sense of unworthiness.
Keil harmonizes the two provisions: 'bread' is general, so Mephibosheth dines with the king yet still provides for his own family from the land Ziba farms.
Henry: the estate is large (cf. 1 Samuel 9:1; 22:7) and Ziba is eyed warily; above all David typifies Christ seeking, restoring, and feasting fallen, lame man.
Clarke: the produce supported the young Micha while the father dined with David, though the whole patrimony belonged to Mephibosheth.
Gill: identifies Ziba as the former servant of Saul now charged with the estate.
Keil: Ziba's fifteen sons and twenty servants form a labour force able to farm the whole estate, probably from Gibeah.
Clarke: Ziba is the 'hind' who works the land at his own cost for half the produce, his interest and duty running together.
Gill: marks that Ziba's sons and servants were many.
Clarke: Ziba's fair, specious words hide a traitor's heart (revealed in Absalom's revolt); character is only shown when circumstances call it out.
Keil: the clause 'as one of the king's sons' is best taken as David's own words, gathered up as the account closes.
Gill: Ziba 'promised very fair, had he been as faithful to his trust.'

themes: fair words versus a false heart

schools.summary: Gill: from Micha sprang a line of several generations; Saul's house, feared extinct, is preserved and continued.
Keil: all who dwelt in Ziba's house are now servants to Mephibosheth.
Clarke: fit for no public office, Mephibosheth is yet treated by the king with utmost respect and affection at his table.
Gill: he now dwells in Jerusalem beside the king, returning no more to Lodebar or the restored estate.
Henry: the lame man kept continually at the table pictures fallen men brought near to God by pure grace.
